title: Listen!
speaker: Pastor Steve Brinegar
source_files: Listen! - GUEST SPEAKER_ Pastor Steve Brinegar - James 1_16-21.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Command to Listen
subpoints: James 1:19 functions like a proverb — swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath
The three commands form an inseparable package for true listening
Illustration: the fire captain whose trained instinct saved his crew
Listening to God's Word must become ingrained in who we are

outline.title: Be Swift to Hear: Positioning Yourself to Receive God's Word
subpoints: Listening is not passive — it requires active engagement and effort
Position yourself daily: church attendance, consistent Bible reading, seeking God's counsel
When facing decisions, go first to the Word of God rather than cultural voices
The Bible is not merely a helpful resource — it is God's authoritative Word

outline.title: Be Slow to Speak: Receiving Rather Than Reacting
subpoints: Speaking breaks the other person's chain of thought and derails true understanding
Our culture is trained for soundbites; we must cultivate patience to hear fully
The Bible as our only rule of faith and practice must not be placed on equal footing with human opinions
Test all teaching and counsel by the Word, as the Bereans did with Paul

outline.title: Be Slow to Wrath: Processing Truth with Patience
subpoints: Anger destroys the ability to reason and negotiate
When difficulty comes, the temptation is to say 'I deserve better' — but we deserve God's wrath
Every good gift comes from above; our trials are light momentary afflictions in light of eternal glory
The wrath of man does not produce the righteousness of God (James 1:20)

outline.title: Practical Application: Filling Mind and Heart with God's Word
subpoints: Fill your mind with God's Word: set a consistent daily time for Scripture reading
Fill your heart with God's Word: store it up as a well-organized toolbox (Psalm 119:11)
All that is evil in man is of himself; all that is good is of God — depend on His Word
We are saved to be the first fruits of His creatures, zealous for good works

summary:
Guest speaker Pastor Steve Brinegar of Grace Bible Presbyterian Church in Cape Canaveral, Florida, preaches on James 1:16-21 with a focus on verse 19: the triple command to be swift to hear, slow to speak, and slow to wrath. Drawing on the illustration of a fire captain whose years of training made him instinctively sense danger, Pastor Brinegar argues that listening to God's Word must become so ingrained in believers that it shapes every decision and response. James 1:19 is not merely practical advice for human communication — it is about how Christians receive the living Word of God.

The sermon develops each of the three commands in turn as they apply to hearing Scripture. Being swift to hear means positioning oneself to receive the Word — through faithful church attendance, daily Bible reading, and running to Scripture first when facing difficult decisions. Being slow to speak means letting the Bible speak before our own opinions and not treating it as merely one voice among many. Being slow to wrath means receiving God's truth with patience rather than reacting when it challenges our sense of what we deserve — for we deserve condemnation, and every good gift, including salvation, is entirely of grace.

Pastor Brinegar grounds the sermon in 2 Corinthians 4:16-18, which reframes earthly afflictions as 'light momentary' compared to an eternal weight of glory. The danger of anger is that it shifts our focus from what the Bible actually says to what we think we deserve. Samuel Miller's summary of Reformed doctrine is quoted: all that is evil in man is of himself; all that is good is of God. The sermon concludes with a call to fill both mind and heart with Scripture daily, using Psalm 119:11 as a picture of storing God's Word as a well-organized toolbox ready for use.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading and Introduction of Guest Speaker

If we're able to remain standing and turn to James chapter 1 for the reading of God's Word. We're going to read verses 16 through 21 of James chapter 1. James 1:16. This is the Word of our Lord:

"Do not be deceived, my beloved brethren. Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, and comes down from the Father of lights, with whom there is no variation or shadow of turning. Of His own will He brought us forth by the word of truth, that we might be a kind of firstfruits of His creatures. So then, my beloved brethren, let every man be swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath; for the wrath of man does not produce the righteousness of God. Therefore lay aside all filthiness and overflow of wickedness, and receive with meekness the implanted word, which is able to save your souls."

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Father in heaven, thank you for your Word. We pray that you be with your messenger this morning to speak through him to our hearts. In Jesus' name, amen. Please be seated.

This morning we welcome to our pulpit Pastor Steve Brinegar. He's the pastor of Grace Bible Presbyterian Church in Cape Canaveral, Florida. He is the Stated Clerk of our Synod, which means he's the one that makes our denomination work in the background as far as everything goes. He's also the chairman of the Board of our seminary, and one of the closest friends I have in this world. So I'm very excited to have him preach to us this morning.

## The Command to Listen

Good morning. It is good to be with you. I do bring greetings from the church in Cape Canaveral. We just hosted Synod, and that was a glorious time for us. You mentioned being Stated Clerk has the greatest job security in the world, because nobody wants to do it, even when I beg.

As we look at the word of God this morning, turn and open to James chapter one if you've closed it. Our main focus is going to be on verse 19.

By way of introduction, I came across a story in a book about thinking — the book is called *Thinking Fast and Slow*. It's about how we have two different parts of our brains we engage for decision making: the fast part, where we make snap decisions like how you walk — you don't think about it, you just do it — and then the slow part, where you make bigger decisions. The authors argue in the book that it's possible to so ingrain and train yourself that when you become a student of something, decisions that should be very complex can become second nature.

By example, they tell the story of a fire captain whose team were fighting a fire in a kitchen, and all of a sudden he yelled for everyone to get out immediately. They stopped what they were doing and got out. Within seconds of them leaving, the floor collapsed. When asked later about why he ordered everybody out, he said, "It was too quiet in there." His experience as a fire captain had trained him that a fire should sound a certain way, and when it didn't sound right, he knew there was danger. It turned out the fire wasn't actually in the kitchen — it was in the basement below.

They say that such expertise in any field — such mastery — can take up to 10,000 hours of work, practice, and training. That's five years of 40 hours a week doing only that — the type of commitment a professional athlete might have to their trade.

As we look at verse 19, James is giving us a command — a very important command: the command to listen. That command needs to become ingrained in who we are, how we think, and how we handle our lives.

Verse 19 serves almost as a proverb — a bit of transition in James chapter 1. It's been said that James is the Proverbs of the New Testament, and verse 19 is a good example of that, where, much like the Proverbs, it has a certain shape to it. It's easy to remember. In fact, if you don't have it memorized now, by the end of the sermon you probably will.

James gives us this command — it's one command, the command to calmly listen — but he issues it in three rapid-fire phrases: be swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath. You notice that there's no connecting word — no "and" — it's just these three commands, all together. The reason he does that is because if you are going to listen, you have to do all three. You can't do one and not the others. It really is a whole package.

## Be Swift to Hear

First, in verse 19, he says that we need to be swift to hear — quick to engage the effort to listen. We have to remember that listening is not a passive activity. Yes, it's true we're not speaking — in a sense you're receiving something — but to do that well you have to be active.

I found that note-taking is a very helpful practice when I'm listening to someone. After church, if somebody comes and tells me something, I usually pull out my phone to put myself a reminder. I carry a little notepad for a while, but I lost that, and so the phone is always with me.

Listening also has a positional aspect to it. If somebody's talking to you across the room and you really want to know what they're saying, the very natural inclination is to move toward them — to get yourself in a position so that you can hear.

Now, this is about much more than the discussions we might have with our neighbors or the debates that might unfold in a meeting of the church. As you read from verse 16 through verse 21 and into the next section of this book, James is talking about not just communicating with one another, but how we receive the Word of God. Look at verse 18: "Of His own will He brought us forth by the word of truth." By the Word of God — the Bible — the means of grace given to us — He has brought us forth.

So positioning yourself to hear God's Word means coming to church, engaging in the time of worship and the preaching of the Word. But it's also what you do every day — having the Bible open every day, reading it, studying it — not just when you're in church.

What do you do when you have to make a big decision — a big life decision? The correct answer is the Word of God. Open your Bible, analyze your problem, find out what the Bible says. If it's a difficulty with a fellow Christian, what does the Bible say about how to handle that conflict? Often the response I get from Christians in such a situation is not "I'll go do that because the Bible says to" but instead "I don't see how that would do any good." When we understand what the Bible says, we need to follow it. We need to obey it.

Not long ago — a little over a month ago — I was going through a frustrating situation and I was discouraged. I looked up 2 Corinthians 4:16-18: "Therefore we do not lose heart. Even though our outward man is perishing, yet the inward man is being renewed day by day. For our light and momentary troubles are achieving for us an eternal glory that far outweighs them all. So we fix our eyes not on what is seen, but on what is unseen, since what is seen is temporary, but what is unseen is eternal."

I had to wrestle with it for several days and ask: what does this mean, and how does it apply to my situation? You might think, "Paul says 'light momentary affliction,' but you don't understand what I'm going through." I'd encourage you to study the book of Acts and see what Paul went through — being stoned to the point they thought he was dead, and then going back into that same city to minister.

The second part of being swift to hear is focus. We have to remember that the Bible is not ultimately a product of human hands — it has no weak points. Any book you pick up, no matter how orthodox and reformed and faithful the author, will have good points and weak points. That is not true of the Word of God. The Bible communicates God's truth to us. It is God's truth. It is God's Word. It is true.

We must not listen to those who seek to undermine the authority of the Word of God. The Word of God is our rule of faith and practice, and we are to test all other teaching by it — as the Bereans did with Paul.

## Be Slow to Speak

The next part is that we must be slow to speak. If you start talking, the other person stops talking — hopefully. You break their chain of thought. We live in a culture trained for soundbites — Twitter, 140 characters, one-sentence snippets. A hundred years ago, debates lasted multiple hours. Today there's not 30 seconds of uninterrupted talk.

We also need to be slow to speak because we need to remember the great point we just thought of. A notepad can be very helpful — write down the point you want to make, then come back to it.

Being slow to speak as we come to the Word of God means not treating it as one voice among many. The Word of God is the authority of God. Those wise counselors in the church who have drunk deeply from the well of God's Word are vitally important — but they are not on the same level as Scripture itself.

## Be Slow to Wrath

Finally, we must be slow to wrath — slow to anger. Just picture a scene of great communication: two parents reaching for the last hot Christmas toy, yelling at the top of their lungs. That's not a scene of good communication. When anger enters in, the ability to reason is gone. People shout the same things over and over with greater intensity. They're not going to negotiate their way to a solution.

When we apply God's Word to a difficult situation — like "your light affliction is working for you an eternal weight of glory" — if our first response is "Yeah, but you don't know how bad I have it," we are not listening. We live in a very consumer-driven society where images tell us every day how much better our lives would be if we just had this or that. But what does the Bible actually teach?

Processing God's truth requires patience. When anger enters in, those questions quickly move from "Can God produce good from this?" to "I deserve better." But we need to be very clear about what we actually deserve. The wages of sin is death. We deserve the eternal curse and wrath of God. By grace — every good gift, every perfect gift — we have salvation and everlasting life.

Verse 20 warns us: "for the wrath of man does not produce the righteousness of God." If we become angry, we're not going to produce the righteousness God requires. We have to seek something outside ourselves — and we do that by listening to God's Word.

Samuel Miller summarized the Reformed faith this way: all that is evil in man is of himself, and to him belongs the blame of it; and all that is good in him is of God, and to God belongs the praise of it. On our own we will be miserable, discouraged, and on our way to death. But with the Lord there is hope, joy, peace, and everlasting life.

## Practical Application

As you think about listening to the Word, there are two things to do. First, fill your minds with God's Word. Start your day with the Word — or if evenings are better, set a consistent time and keep it. If you wait for leftover time, you will not have leftover time.

Second — and a little more difficult — fill your heart with God's Word. Psalm 119:11: "Your word I have hidden in my heart, that I might not sin against You." The picture is a well-organized toolbox — labeled, accessible, always ready. That's memorization, yes, but also knowing the Word and being able to use it.

May we walk with the Lord each and every day. May we fill our minds and our hearts with His Word, that we might be the firstfruits of His creatures.

Let us pray. Father, we thank You for Your Word. Thank You that we can study it together. I pray, Father, that You would cause us to look to You in all things, to serve You faithfully. Father, may Your Word be our only rule of faith and practice. We ask in Jesus' name. Amen.
